scripts.title: Meta vs Google for service businesses
scripts.film: Film sitting down, natural lighting, direct eye contact with camera. Relaxed posture. No background distractions — just you and the camera. Let your hands move naturally when explaining the differences.

captions.label: A
captions.text: meta wins for service businesses. here's why

captions.label: B
captions.text:
meta vs google
meta: people browsing, looking for solutions
google: people actively searching, ready to call

captions.label: C
captions.text: for most service businesses at $25/day, meta converts better

lines.text: everyone asks me — should I run Meta or Google ads? the honest answer might surprise you

Google is intent-based. Someone types 'plumber near me' — they want you now. But the cost per click is brutal. You're competing with every other plumber in your area. And at $25 a day? You run out of budget in hours.

Meta is different. People aren't searching for a plumber. They're scrolling, they see your ad, and if it's good — they call. The cost per lead is way lower. Your $25 goes further. And you're not in a bidding war with fifty competitors.
This doesn't mean Google is bad. But if you've got a tight budget and you're just starting out, Meta lets you actually test and scale without bleeding money.
Pick the platform that fits your budget and your market. For most service businesses? That's Meta.

scripts.title: 3 reasons your Meta ads aren't getting leads
scripts.film: Screen record Meta Ads Manager on desktop. Start zoomed in on the Campaigns tab showing a live ad account. As you talk through each mistake, navigate to relevant sections (Audience, Ad Creative, Landing Page) to show real examples. Keep cursor movements smooth and deliberate. Speak at a natural pace like you're walking a friend through their account.

captions.label: A
captions.text: most people fix the wrong thing and wonder why nothing changes

captions.label: B
captions.text: your audience is too broad, your ad looks like everything else, or you're sending them nowhere to actually book. fix those three things and watch what happens

captions.label: C
captions.text: these three mistakes kill 90% of service business ad accounts

lines.text: if your Meta ads aren't generating leads, you're probably making one of these three mistakes — and most people don't even know which one

first one: your audience is way too broad. you're targeting everyone in a 50-mile radius when you should be narrowing it down to neighborhoods where your price point actually works. people in the wealthy suburbs want different service providers than people three miles away. get specific.

second: your creative doesn't stand out. people scroll past hundreds of ads a day. if your ad looks like a stock photo with generic text, it's invisible. show real before-and-afters, show your team, show the actual problem you solve. make someone stop.

third one's the killer: you're sending them to your homepage or nowhere at all. people click your ad and land on a generic page that doesn't match what they just saw. they leave immediately. send them to a page built for that specific service, specific problem, specific call-to-action.
those three things account for like 90 percent of dead ad accounts. fix all three and you're already ahead of most people out there

scripts.title: Window cleaner: 0 to 11 jobs in 30 days
scripts.film: Film Marco sitting down, relaxed posture, natural lighting from the side. Direct eye contact with camera. Let him smile when he says '11 jobs' — this is a genuine win worth celebrating.

captions.label: A
captions.text: window cleaner went from zero to 11 booked jobs in his first month running meta ads at $25 a day

captions.label: B
captions.text: this window cleaner had no ad experience. didn't know where to start. $25 a day budget. now he's booked out. here's what we did different

captions.label: C
captions.text: the secret wasn't the budget. it was the targeting.

lines.text: This window cleaner had literally never run an ad before. Zero experience. We started him at $25 a day. One month later, he's got 11 booked jobs.
Here's what most people get wrong — they think the budget's the problem. It's not. He proved that. The issue is who you're actually talking to.
We targeted homeowners in his service area who'd actually searched for window cleaning recently. Not a broad audience. Specific people, right time, right message.
His ad was simple too. Just his before-and-afters, the price, and a clear way to book. No fluff. People saw it, recognized they needed him, and booked.
Twenty-five dollars a day works when you're not wasting money on people who don't want what you're selling.

scripts.title: $25/day ad budget breakdown for service businesses
scripts.film: Film at your desk or in a clean space. Use a simple spreadsheet or notes app on screen showing the breakdown numbers. Keep it visual and easy to read — this is about making the math real and believable.

captions.label: A
captions.text: this is what $25/day actually gets you as a plumber or window cleaner. the math checks out.

captions.label: B
captions.text: people ask what a $25/day budget produces. here's the real breakdown — leads, costs, and what matters.

captions.label: C
captions.text: $25/day isn't small money. here's exactly where it goes.

lines.text: so you're thinking about running ads at $25 a day. let me show you exactly what that actually produces.
$25 a day is $750 a month. on Meta, you're usually looking at a cost per lead between $3 and $8 depending on your market and business type. that's roughly 95 to 250 leads a month.

not all leads convert, right? typical close rate for service businesses is around 10 to 20 percent. so at the lower end, you're looking at maybe 10 to 15 jobs booked from that budget. at the higher end, closer to 30 or 40.
average job value for most trades is somewhere between $400 and $2000. even at the conservative end, 10 jobs at $600 each is $6000 in revenue. that's a nine to one return. not bad from $750.
$25 a day works because you're not trying to hit everyone — you're hitting the people in your area who actually need your service right now. that's the whole game.

scripts.title: One word change that kills CTR
scripts.film: Film Marco sitting down, relaxed but engaged, in a bright space. Natural light. He should lean in slightly when he says the word change — make it feel like he's sharing a real discovery, not reading.

captions.label: A
captions.text: the word 'free' tanks your click rate. 'no cost' works way better.

captions.label: B
captions.text: people see 'free' and think low quality or spam. swap it for 'no cost' and your clicks go up. tested this on hundreds of plumber and cleaner ads.

captions.label: C
captions.text: 'free' feels cheap. 'no cost' feels real.

lines.text: There's one word in your ad that's killing your click-through rate and you probably don't even know it.
The word is 'free.' I know it sounds backwards, right? Like everyone wants free stuff. But here's the thing — people see 'free' and their brain immediately thinks low quality or spam.
So instead, use 'no cost.' That's it. Same message, completely different vibe. It sounds professional. It sounds like a real business offer, not a gimmick.
I've tested this on window cleaner ads, plumber ads, HVAC guys — the ones using 'no cost inspection' or 'no cost quote' consistently get more clicks than 'free.' It's small, but it works.
One word swap. Better CTR. That's how you optimize without changing your whole strategy.

scripts.title: The campaign objective mistake costing you leads
scripts.film: Film in your office or workspace, natural lighting, sit down casual. Lean in slightly when you hit the hook and main points — shows confidence and urgency without being forced.

captions.label: A
captions.text: most service businesses optimize for the wrong thing and wonder why their ads don't convert

captions.label: B
captions.text: you're probably optimizing for clicks when you should be optimizing for leads. two totally different things.

captions.label: C
captions.text: clicks ≠ leads. optimize for the right thing or burn cash

lines.text: most plumbers, window cleaners, and HVAC guys I work with are running ads optimized for clicks. that's the problem.
see, clicks are cheap. Meta can get you a ton of them for $25 a day. but cheap clicks don't mean cheap leads. they usually mean low-intent traffic that's not ready to book you.
when you optimize for leads instead, Meta learns who actually fills out your form, who actually calls, who's actually a real prospect. and then it finds more people like them.
so you end up spending less per qualified lead because Meta's not just chasing random clicks anymore. it's chasing the conversions that actually matter to your business.
small shift in how you set up the campaign. huge difference in what you actually pay per lead.

scripts.title: Why your ads die after 3 days
scripts.film: Film Marco sitting down, relaxed, looking directly at camera. Natural indoor lighting. He should gesture lightly when explaining the three points — keep it conversational and grounded, not over-the-top.

captions.label: A
captions.text: your ad frequency is maxing out before your audience sees it enough times

captions.label: B
captions.text: your audience is too small so everyone sees it by day 3. then there's nobody left to show it to. fix: expand your radius or widen your targeting slightly

captions.label: C
captions.text: most people aren't ready to buy on day one. they need to see you 3-4 times first

lines.text: Your ad is crushing for three days then just… stops. You know why? It's not because your ad sucks.

First thing — your audience pool is probably too small. You're running at like a 2-mile radius with targeting so tight that everyone in that group has already seen your ad twice by day three. There's nobody left.

Second thing — frequency burnout. People aren't ready to call a plumber or book a detail on the first impression. Most of your leads come after someone's seen you three, four, sometimes five times. But if your audience is small, you hit that ceiling fast.

The fix is simple. Expand your radius by half a mile or a mile. Loosen your targeting just slightly — age range, interests, whatever you had locked down. Let the algorithm run longer before everyone in your pool burns out.
Your ad isn't dying because it's bad. It's dying because your audience saw all of it too fast. Fix the pool, fix the problem.
